summaries:
The team has initiated an authentication system rebuild under the tracking label `auth-rebuild-design`, structured in four phases: schema design, token issuance, validation middleware, and cutover. The core architectural decision was to adopt JWT with HS256 signing over an OAuth-only approach, driven by the need for stateless validation at the edge to support a peak load of 12k requests per second without session-store roundtrips. The token strategy uses 15-minute access tokens paired with 7-day refresh tokens, with the JWT signing key stored in SSM Parameter Store at /molt/prod/jwt-signing-key, encrypted with KMS and rotated quarterly.

The existing authentication endpoint is /api/v1/auth/login, which currently issues a cookie named molt_session with HttpOnly and SameSite=Lax attributes. The JWT payload includes standard claims (sub for user_id, iss as auth.example.com, aud as api.example.com, exp, iat, jti as UUID) plus custom claims for amr (set to ['mfa'] when multi-factor authentication is passed) and tier (free/pro/enterprise). The SameSite=Lax setting was chosen because the application doesn't rely on cross-site POSTs for authentication flows, making the stricter Strict policy unnecessary.

Refresh token security employs a sliding window rotation strategy where each refresh operation issues a new refresh token and blacklists the old one in a Redis short-TTL set. The blacklist uses the existing molt-cache-prod Redis cluster (cluster mode, 3 shards, Multi-AZ) under the keyspace auth:refresh:blacklist:* with an 8-day TTL. When refresh operations fail, the system returns a 401 status with a WWW-Authenticate: Bearer error="invalid_token" header, prompting the client to redirect to /login. If the Redis cluster becomes unavailable, the blacklist check fails open (accepting the refresh token) while triggering a paging alert, with the option to switch to an in-memory fallback for brief outages.

The system includes comprehensive logging at INFO level for successful operations, WARN for refresh failures, and ERROR for signing failures, all in JSON-structured format. Audit logs are retained for 90 days hot in CloudWatch and 7 years cold in S3 Glacier to meet compliance requirements. Testing coverage includes unit tests on the validation middleware achieving 95% coverage and end-to-end Playwright tests covering the complete login, refresh, and revoke flow. Error messages support internationalization with keys for English, Spanish, French, and Japanese. A known Safari 16 issue with HttpOnly cookies in private mode is addressed with a sessionStorage fallback accompanied by a security warning in the UI. The security review confirmed that the rate-limit Redis store is highly available, backed by the same molt-cache-prod multi-AZ cluster. A penetration test is planned with Cobalt once the staging environment has a full token flow deployed.

The team has moved from design into implementation of the authentication token issuance system, renaming the thread from `auth-rebuild-design` to `auth-token-issuance` to reflect this transition. The schema work is complete, and development has begun on the new token issuance endpoint at /api/v2/auth/token. This endpoint accepts POST requests with username, password, and mfa_code, returning both access and refresh tokens while setting a molt_session cookie.

A critical security decision was made to support TOTP-only multi-factor authentication via authenticator apps, explicitly excluding SMS-based MFA due to interception risks and US carrier reliability concerns for a financial application. For SSO users, the system trusts the SAML assertion's amr claim to indicate that MFA has already been enforced at the identity provider layer, avoiding redundant prompts. The JWT signing infrastructure has been upgraded with a new key stored at /molt/prod/jwt-signing-key-v2, encrypted using KMS multi-region key mrk-1234abcd5678efgh, with the old key retained for a seven-day grace period.

The JWT payload structure includes standard claims: sub for user_id, iss set to auth.example.com, aud set to api.example.com, along with exp, iat, and a UUID-based jti. It also carries an amr claim set to ['mfa'] when MFA is passed and a tier claim indicating the user's subscription level (free/pro/enterprise). Token revocation is handled via POST /api/v2/auth/revoke, which adds the JTI to a Redis blacklist and revokes the entire refresh token chain. When refresh attempts fail, the system returns a 401 status with a WWW-Authenticate: Bearer error="invalid_token" header, triggering a client-side redirect to /login.

The molt_session cookie uses SameSite=Lax, deemed sufficient since no critical cross-site POST operations exist for authentication. A known Safari 16 issue with HttpOnly cookies in private mode is addressed through a fallback to sessionStorage, accompanied by a security warning in the UI. Comprehensive audit logging captures the amr claim value, IP address, user agent, and success/failure status to CloudWatch log group /molt/prod/auth-audit, with logs retained for 90 days hot in CloudWatch and seven years cold in S3 Glacier to meet compliance requirements. Logging levels are structured as INFO for success paths, WARN for refresh failures, and ERROR for signing failures, all in JSON format.

Testing coverage includes unit tests on validation middleware achieving 95% coverage and end-to-end Playwright tests covering the complete login, refresh, and revoke flow. Error messages support internationalization with keys for English, Spanish, French, and Japanese. The system includes resilience planning: if Redis becomes unavailable, the refresh token blacklist fails open (accepting refresh tokens) while triggering a paging alert, with the option to switch to an in-memory fallback for brief outages. The rate-limit Redis store has been confirmed as highly available, backed by molt-cache-prod running in a multi-AZ configuration. All security review items have been addressed.

The conversation covers the completion of token issuance work and the subsequent split into two parallel threads: auth-validation-middleware and auth-mfa-flow. The team selected jose as the JWT validation library over jsonwebtoken due to its superior TypeScript types and native use of node:crypto without additional dependencies. CORS configuration allows origins from https://app.example.com and https://admin.example.com with credentials enabled to support cookie-based flows.

Rate limiting on the token endpoint is set at 60 requests per minute per IP with a burst capacity of 100, implemented using express-rate-limit with a Redis store running on molt-cache-prod. The system emits three key metrics to Datadog with the env:prod tag: auth.token.issued.count, auth.token.revoked.count, and auth.mfa.failure.count. The validation middleware has reached feature-complete status in staging and is awaiting penetration testing, while the MFA flow is active with completed UI hooks and a live backend QR-code endpoint, though the TOTP secret rotation flow remains pending.

Cobalt has been engaged for penetration testing with a 5-day window starting Monday, involving two testers. When refresh tokens fail, the system returns a 401 status with a WWW-Authenticate: Bearer error="invalid_token" header, triggering a client-side redirect to /login. Logging follows a structured JSON format with INFO level for success paths, WARN for refresh failures, and ERROR for signing failures. The JWT payload includes sub (user_id), iss (auth.example.com), aud (api.example.com), exp, iat, jti (UUID), amr (['mfa'] when MFA passed), and tier (free/pro/enterprise).

Cookies use SameSite=Lax rather than Strict since there are no cross-site POSTs critical to authentication. A known Safari 16 issue with HttpOnly cookies in private mode is addressed through a fallback to sessionStorage accompanied by a security warning in the UI. Testing coverage includes unit tests on the validation middleware achieving 95% coverage and end-to-end Playwright tests covering the complete login, refresh, and revoke flow. Error messages support internationalization with current translations in English, Spanish, French, and Japanese.

The refresh token blacklist has a fail-open strategy if Redis becomes unavailable, accepting refresh tokens while triggering a paging alert, with the option to switch to an in-memory fallback for brief outages. Audit logs are retained for 90 days hot in CloudWatch and 7 years cold in S3 Glacier to meet compliance requirements. The single outstanding security review item concerns confirming the rate-limit Redis store's high availability, which has been verified as the molt-cache-prod instance is multi-AZ configured.

The conversation covers the final stages of an authentication system v2 launch and the deprecation of v1. Version 2 went GA yesterday, with a deprecation banner now live for v1 users and a sunset date set for 2026-08-01. A recent pen-test identified one medium-severity CSRF vulnerability on the /api/v2/auth/revoke endpoint, which has already been patched using a double-submit cookie pattern. A new thread called auth-deprecation-comms was created to handle customer-facing communications, including drafting emails and changelog posts for the v1 deprecation.

The technical implementation details are well-defined. JWT tokens include standard claims like sub (user_id), iss (auth.example.com), aud (api.example.com), exp, iat, and jti (UUID), plus custom fields for amr (set to ['mfa'] when MFA is passed) and tier (free/pro/enterprise). Refresh token failures return a 401 status with a WWW-Authenticate: Bearer error="invalid_token" header, triggering a client-side redirect to /login. Cookies use SameSite=Lax, which is sufficient since there are no critical cross-site POST operations. A known Safari 16 issue with HttpOnly cookies in private mode is handled via a fallback to sessionStorage, accompanied by a security warning in the UI.

Logging follows a tiered approach: INFO for successful operations, WARN for refresh failures, and ERROR for signing failures, all in JSON-structured format. Testing coverage is robust, with unit tests achieving 95% coverage on validation middleware and end-to-end Playwright tests covering the complete login, refresh, and revoke flows. Error messages support internationalization with keys for English, Spanish, French, and Japanese.

Infrastructure resilience is addressed through several mechanisms. The refresh token blacklist uses Redis, and if Redis goes down, the system fails open (accepting refresh tokens) while triggering a paging alert; an in-memory fallback is available for short outages. Audit logs are retained for 90 days hot in CloudWatch and 7 years cold in S3 Glacier to meet compliance requirements. The rate-limit Redis store is backed by molt-cache-prod, which is multi-AZ and highly available, resolving the one outstanding security review item.

metas.summary:
This session focused on designing a JWT-based authentication rebuild for a high-throughput API (12k rps peak). The core architectural decision was to use JWT with HS256 signing rather than OAuth-only, driven by the need for stateless validation at the edge cache layer. Token lifetimes were set at 15 minutes for access tokens and 7 days for refresh tokens, with a sliding-window refresh strategy that issues new refresh tokens on each refresh and blacklists old ones in Redis.

The implementation leverages existing infrastructure: the JWT signing key lives in SSM Parameter Store (`/molt/prod/jwt-signing-key`), encrypted with KMS and rotated quarterly. The refresh token blacklist uses a dedicated keyspace (`auth:refresh:blacklist:*`) in the existing `molt-cache-prod` Redis cluster (cluster mode, 3 shards, Multi-AZ) with an 8-day TTL. If Redis goes down, the system fails open (accepts refresh tokens) but triggers a paging alert, with an option to fall back to in-memory storage for short outages. The current login endpoint is `/api/v1/auth/login`, and the session cookie is named `molt_session` with HttpOnly and SameSite=Lax attributes.

The JWT payload includes standard claims (sub, iss, aud, exp, iat, jti) plus custom fields for MFA status (`amr`) and user tier (free/pro/enterprise). Error handling returns 401 with `WWW-Authenticate: Bearer error="invalid_token"` headers on refresh failures, prompting client-side redirect to `/login`. Logging is JSON-structured with INFO for success, WARN for refresh failures, and ERROR for signing failures. Audit logs are retained 90 days hot in CloudWatch and 7 years cold in S3 Glacier for compliance.

Browser compatibility includes a fallback for Safari 16's HttpOnly cookie issue in private mode, using sessionStorage with a security warning. Error messages support i18n in English, Spanish, French, and Japanese. Testing coverage includes 95% unit test coverage on validation middleware and end-to-end Playwright tests for the full login/refresh/revoke flow. A pen-test engagement with Cobalt is planned once staging deployment is complete. The rate-limit Redis store was confirmed to be HA (backed by the same multi-AZ `molt-cache-prod` cluster), closing the final open security review item.

metas.summary:
This session focused on implementing the JWT token issuance endpoint at `/api/v2/auth/token`, which accepts POST requests with username, password, and optional MFA code, returning both access and refresh tokens while setting the `molt_session` cookie. The thread was renamed from `auth-rebuild-design` to `auth-token-issuance` to reflect the shift from design to implementation. A key security decision was made to support TOTP-only MFA via authenticator apps, explicitly rejecting SMS-based MFA due to interception risks and carrier reliability concerns for a financial application.

The JWT signing key was rotated to a new multi-region KMS key (`mrk-1234abcd5678efgh`) with the new SSM path `/molt/prod/jwt-signing-key-v2`, keeping the old key active for a 7-day grace period. For SSO users, the system trusts the SAML assertion's `amr` claim to indicate MFA was already enforced at the IdP layer, avoiding double prompts. All authentication events are logged to the CloudWatch log group `/molt/prod/auth-audit`, including the `amr` value, IP address, user agent, and success/failure status.

A token revocation endpoint was implemented at `POST /api/v2/auth/revoke`, which adds the JWT's JTI to the Redis blacklist and revokes the entire refresh token chain. The JWT payload structure includes standard claims (sub, iss, aud, exp, iat, jti) plus custom fields for MFA status (`amr` array) and user tier (free/pro/enterprise). Error handling, logging levels, browser compatibility (including Safari 16 private mode fallback), testing coverage, internationalization support, Redis failover strategy, and audit log retention policies all remain consistent with the previous compaction's specifications.

metas.summary:
This session focused on implementing the JWT validation middleware and polishing the MFA flow, splitting the previous `auth-token-issuance` thread into two new sub-threads. The validation middleware is now feature-complete in staging and uses the `jose` npm library (chosen over `jsonwebtoken` for better TypeScript types and native node:crypto support). CORS is configured to allow credentials from `https://app.example.com` and `https://admin.example.com`. Rate limiting on the token endpoint is set at 60 requests per minute per IP with a burst of 100, implemented via `express-rate-limit` backed by the existing `molt-cache-prod` Redis cluster.

The MFA flow is active with UI hooks complete and a live QR-code endpoint, pending implementation of the TOTP secret rotation flow. A pen-test engagement with Cobalt is scheduled for a 5-day window starting Monday with two testers. Metrics are being emitted to Datadog (`auth.token.issued.count`, `auth.token.revoked.count`, `auth.mfa.failure.count`) with the `env:prod` tag.

All previously established patterns remain consistent: refresh failures return 401 with `WWW-Authenticate: Bearer error="invalid_token"` headers, logging uses INFO/WARN/ERROR levels with JSON structure, JWT payload includes standard and custom claims (amr, tier), cookies use SameSite=Lax, Safari 16 private mode has a sessionStorage fallback with security warning, test coverage is 95% on validation middleware with end-to-end Playwright tests, error messages support i18n in four languages, Redis failover strategy fails open with paging alerts, and audit logs retain 90 days hot in CloudWatch and 7 years cold in S3 Glacier. The final security review item (confirming rate-limit Redis store HA) was closed by verifying the multi-AZ configuration of `molt-cache-prod`.

metas.summary:
This session focused on post-GA activities following the v2 authentication system launch. The pen-test report from Cobalt identified one medium-severity CSRF vulnerability on the `/api/v2/auth/revoke` endpoint, which was immediately patched using a double-submit cookie pattern. The v2 API was declared GA as of yesterday, with a deprecation banner shipped for v1 and a sunset date set for August 1, 2026. A new thread was opened to handle customer-facing communications around the v1 deprecation, including drafting customer emails and changelog posts.

The conversation included extensive review of the existing authentication system's technical details, confirming all previously established patterns remain in place. All technical implementation threads (JWT validation middleware, MFA flow) are now completed, with the revoke endpoint CSRF patch also finished. The most active work is now the deprecation communications thread, shifting focus from implementation to customer transition planning.

conv-auth-launch.question: Why did we choose JWT instead of OAuth-only for auth?
conv-auth-launch.expectedAnswer: Stateless validation fits the edge-cache pattern; no session-store roundtrip

conv-auth-launch.question: What was the original cookie name we used in v1?
Where did we store the original JWT signing key?

kasett.answer: /molt/prod/jwt-signing-key-v2 in SSM Parameter Store, encrypted with multi-region KMS key mrk-1234abcd5678efgh.

conv-auth-launch.question: Why did we choose TOTP over SMS for MFA?
conv-auth-launch.expectedAnswer: SMS interception risk and US carrier reliability problems

conv-auth-launch.question: What is the new KMS key ID we used for the v2 signing key?
What was the original name of the thread when it was about design (before we renamed it during issuance work)?
Why did we choose the `jose` library over `jsonwebtoken` for JWT validation?
conv-auth-launch.expectedAnswer: Better TypeScript types and uses node:crypto natively

kasett.answer: We chose `jose` for its TypeScript types and native node:crypto support.

conv-auth-launch.question: What is the rate limit on the /api/v2/auth/token endpoint?
What two threads did `auth-token-issuance` split into?
What was the medium-severity finding from the pen-test?

vanilla.answer: A CSRF vulnerability on the /api/v2/auth/revoke endpoint.

conv-auth-launch.question: What is the v1 sunset date?
What is the most active thread right now?

kasett.answer: The most active thread right now is the v1 deprecation customer communications and changelog (thread ID: auth-deprecation-comms).
